zotero.abstractNote: Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection is a crucial task for the safe deployment of machine learning models. A large class of OOD detection methods relies on training a model on both in-distribution (ID) and OOD data. In this paper, we show that many of these methods, despite their different motivations and architectures, are essentially estimating the same core quantities: the ID-class conditional densities and the OOD density. We demonstrate that these methods can be decomposed into a combination of these densities and a classifier. This perspective allows us to unify a wide range of existing approaches, explain their performance, and derive a simple yet powerful baseline that outperforms many complex state-of-the-art methods. We provide extensive experiments on standard benchmarks to support our theoretical findings.
